companionParts.description: Buck converter for efficient step-down conversion
Optional RC filter for ultra-low noise

faqs.question: What is PSRR and why is it important for LDOs?
faqs.answer: PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio) measures how well an LDO rejects noise and ripple from the input power supply. It is expressed in dB, with higher values indicating better rejection. The PSLDO50 achieves 80dB PSRR at 1kHz, meaning it attenuates input noise by a factor of 10,000 at that frequency. This is critical for sensitive analog circuits like ADCs, DACs, and RF systems that require clean power. High PSRR prevents switching noise from upstream converters from affecting analog performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select LDOs with PSRR >60dB at your critical frequencies. The PSLDO50 excels at audio and ADC sampling frequencies.

faqs.question: How does dropout voltage affect LDO performance?
faqs.answer: Dropout voltage is the minimum input-output voltage differential required for the LDO to maintain regulation. The PSLDO50 has a low dropout of 200mV at 500mA, allowing it to regulate even when the input is only 200mV above the desired output. This is important for battery-powered applications where maximizing battery life requires operating until the battery voltage drops close to the output voltage. Lower dropout voltage extends battery life and reduces power dissipation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose LDOs with dropout voltage lower than your minimum input-output headroom. The PSLDO50 is ideal for 3.3V output from Li-ion batteries.

faqs.question: What capacitors are recommended for the PSLDO50?
faqs.answer: For the PSLDO50, use a 1uF to 10uF ceramic output capacitor with X5R or X7R dielectric for stability. The capacitor should be placed close to the output pin. For improved transient response, use larger output capacitance (22uF to 47uF). An optional 10nF bypass capacitor at the BYP pin reduces output noise. Use low-ESR ceramic capacitors for best performance. The input capacitor (1uF to 10uF) helps reject input noise and should be placed close to the input pin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 10uF output and 1uF input capacitance. Add bypass capacitor for lowest noise. Our FAE team can optimize for your specific requirements.

faqs.question: How do I minimize output noise from the PSLDO50?
faqs.answer: To minimize output noise: 1) Use the BYP pin with a 10nF capacitor to ground - this creates a low-pass filter for the internal reference; 2) Use larger output capacitance (22uF or more) to filter high-frequency noise; 3) Keep the PCB layout clean with short traces and good grounding; 4) Use an additional RC filter (10Ω + 10uF) at the output for ultra-sensitive applications; 5) Ensure the input supply is clean - the LDO can only reject noise, not eliminate it. These techniques can achieve output noise below 20uVrms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use bypass capacitor for best noise performance. For ultra-low noise, add external RC filter. Contact our FAE team for noise-critical applications.

faqs.question: What is the thermal performance of the PSLDO50?
faqs.answer: The PSLDO50 includes thermal shutdown protection at 165°C junction temperature with 15°C hysteresis. The maximum power dissipation depends on the package and thermal resistance. For the SOT-89 package with thermal resistance of 150°C/W, the maximum power dissipation is about 600mW at 25°C ambient. For higher currents or larger dropout voltages, use the DFN package with better thermal performance or add a small heatsink. Calculate junction temperature using: Tj = Ta + (Pd × Rthja), where Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation and junction temperature for your application. Use DFN package for higher power or contact our FAE team for thermal solutions.

products.name: 12V 3A Synchronous Buck Converter
products.shortDescription: Compact 3A buck converter optimized for 12V industrial applications with high efficiency
descriptionParagraphs: The PSP1203 is a high-efficiency synchronous buck converter optimized for 12V input applications.
With 3A output current capability and up to 96% efficiency, this converter is ideal for industrial control systems, networking equipment, and distributed power architectures.
The device features fixed 500kHz switching frequency, internal compensation, and comprehensive protection features.

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: The PSP1203 is specifically optimized for 12V industrial applications where efficiency and compact size are critical. The fixed 500kHz frequency provides a good balance between efficiency and solution size. I've used this converter in networking equipment where the 96% efficiency minimizes heat generation in enclosed spaces. The internal compensation simplifies design - you don't need to calculate external compensation components. The SOT-23-6 package is very compact for a 3A converter. For best EMI performance, I recommend using a shielded inductor and placing input capacitors as close as possible to the IC.
faeReview.highlight: Optimized for 12V with 96% efficiency in compact package

products.name: 1.8V 300mA Ultra-Low Noise LDO
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low noise 300mA LDO with 90dB PSRR, designed for RF and precision analog applications
descriptionParagraphs: The PSLDO18 is an ultra-low noise 300mA LDO regulator designed for noise-sensitive RF and precision analog applications.
With 90dB PSRR at 1kHz and only 10μVrms output noise, this LDO provides exceptionally clean power for high-performance circuits.
The device features fast transient response, low dropout voltage of 150mV at 300mA, and enable function for power management.

faeReview.author: Jennifer Liu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Analog Power
faeReview.content: The PSLDO18 is my top choice for ultra-sensitive analog and RF applications. The 10uVrms noise level is exceptional - I've used this LDO to power high-resolution ADCs where every microvolt of noise matters. The 90dB PSRR at 1kHz effectively rejects switching noise from upstream converters. The fast transient response handles load steps from RF power amplifiers without significant voltage droop. For best noise performance, I recommend adding a 10nF bypass capacitor at the BYP pin and keeping the output capacitor close to the load. The low quiescent current is also great for battery-powered portable equipment.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low 10uVrms noise with 90dB PSRR for sensitive applications

categories.name: Motor Drivers
categories.description: Integrated motor driver ICs for stepper, DC, and brushless DC motors
categories.longDescription: PrideSilicon's motor driver portfolio provides integrated solutions for controlling various motor types in industrial and automotive applications. Our motor drivers feature high current capability, comprehensive protection features, and flexible control interfaces to meet the requirements of modern motion control systems. The product line includes H-bridge drivers for DC motors, stepper motor drivers with microstepping capability, and three-phase BLDC motor drivers with sensorless control. All motor drivers support wide voltage ranges and offer protection features including overcurrent, overtemperature, and short-circuit protection. Key features include current capability up to 5A, microstepping up to 1/32 step, integrated current sensing, and industrial and automotive temperature grades. As an authorized PrideSilicon distributor, we provide comprehensive technical support including reference designs, application notes, and motor control design services to help you achieve optimal system performance.

selectionGuide.title: Motor Driver Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right motor driver for your application
recommendations: Use H-bridge drivers for DC motors
Use stepper drivers for precision positioning
Use BLDC drivers for high-efficiency applications

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right PrideSilicon motor driver?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting PrideSilicon motor drivers based on motor type, current requirements, and application needs

faqs.question: What types of motor drivers does PrideSilicon offer?
faqs.answer: PrideSilicon offers three main types of motor drivers: H-Bridge Drivers for DC motors with bidirectional control and PWM speed regulation; Stepper Motor Drivers with microstepping capability up to 1/32 step for precision positioning; and Three-Phase BLDC Motor Drivers with sensorless control for high-efficiency applications. H-bridge drivers are ideal for simple DC motor control. Stepper drivers excel in precision positioning systems. BLDC drivers provide highest efficiency for continuous rotation applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose H-bridge for DC motors, stepper drivers for precision positioning, BLDC drivers for high-efficiency continuous rotation. Contact our FAE team for motor control guidance.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the current requirements for my motor driver?
faqs.answer: Motor driver current requirements depend on motor specifications and application demands. Calculate the RMS current based on motor torque requirements and duty cycle. For DC motors, the stall current is typically 5-10x the rated current - ensure the driver can handle this briefly. For stepper motors, calculate current based on desired torque and motor inductance. PrideSilicon motor drivers feature adjustable current limiting to protect both the driver and motor. Always include margin above calculated requirements for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate worst-case current requirements including stall conditions. Select drivers with at least 20% current margin. Our FAE team can help with current calculations.

faqs.question: What is microstepping and when should I use it?
faqs.answer: Microstepping divides each full step of a stepper motor into smaller increments, providing smoother motion and finer positioning resolution. PrideSilicon stepper drivers support microstepping up to 1/32 step, providing 6400 steps per revolution for a standard 200-step motor. Benefits include reduced vibration and noise, smoother motion at low speeds, and higher positioning resolution. Use microstepping for applications requiring smooth motion, low noise, or high positioning resolution. Trade-offs include reduced torque at high microstep settings and increased computational requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1/16 or 1/32 microstepping for smooth motion and low noise. Use full or half stepping for maximum torque. Contact our FAE team for microstepping optimization.

faqs.question: How do I protect my motor driver from electrical transients?
faqs.answer: Motor drivers are susceptible to electrical transients from motor inductance and switching. Protection measures include: 1) Use TVS diodes or varistors across the motor supply to clamp voltage spikes; 2) Add flyback diodes for DC motors to recirculate inductive current; 3) Use bulk capacitance near the driver to supply surge current; 4) Implement RC snubbers across motor terminals for high-speed switching; 5) Ensure proper PCB layout with short, wide traces for high-current paths. PrideSilicon drivers include internal protection, but external components provide additional safety margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement comprehensive protection for industrial applications. Our FAE team can provide protection circuit recommendations for your specific motor and load.

faqs.question: What are the key PCB layout considerations for motor drivers?
faqs.answer: Proper PCB layout is critical for motor driver performance and reliability. Key recommendations include: 1) Use wide, short traces for high-current motor connections; 2) Place bulk capacitance close to the driver power pins; 3) Separate high-current motor ground from sensitive signal ground; 4) Keep switching nodes away from sensitive analog circuits; 5) Use thermal vias under the driver package for heat dissipation; 6) Implement proper current sensing layout for accurate measurement. Following these guidelines ensures reliable operation and long driver lifetime.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow layout guidelines for reliable motor control. Our FAE team can review your PCB layout and provide optimization recommendations.

products.name: Dual H-Bridge DC Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Dual H-bridge motor driver with 2A per channel, PWM control, current sensing for DC motor applications
descriptionParagraphs: The PSD8826 is a dual H-bridge motor driver capable of driving two DC motors or one stepper motor with up to 2A per channel.
With low RDS(on) of 0.3Ω per switch and integrated current sensing, this driver provides efficient motor control for robotics and automation applications.
The device features PWM speed control, overcurrent protection, thermal shutdown, and multiple interface options including parallel and serial control.
features: Dual H-bridge for two DC motors or one stepper
Low RDS(on) of 0.3Ω for high efficiency
PWM speed control up to 100 kHz

faeReview.author: Robert Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Control
faeReview.content: The PSD8826 is my go-to recommendation for DC motor control applications. The low RDS(on) of 0.3Ω keeps power dissipation low even at 2A continuous current. I particularly like the integrated current sensing - it eliminates the need for external sense resistors and simplifies current control loops. I've used this driver in robotics applications where the 100 kHz PWM frequency allows smooth speed control without audible noise. The protection features are comprehensive and have saved several designs from damage during testing. For best performance, I recommend using short, wide traces for motor connections and placing bulk capacitance close to the driver.
faeReview.highlight: Low RDS(on) with integrated current sensing for efficient motor control

alternativeParts.comparison: PSD8826=><TB6612FNG: Output current 1.2A < 2A (PrideSilicon higher current), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative for low-voltage applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Suitable for battery-powered toys and small robotics

alternativeParts.comparison: PSD8826=><DRV8833: Output current 1.5A < 2A (PrideSilicon higher current), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Compact alternative for low-voltage battery applications

companionParts.description: Buck converter for motor supply voltage

faqs.question: What is the maximum current the PSD8826 can deliver?
faqs.answer: The PSD8826 can deliver up to 2A continuous current per channel with adequate thermal management. The device can handle 3A peak current for short durations (typically less than 1 second). The actual maximum current depends on thermal conditions - with good heatsinking and airflow, the driver can sustain 2A continuously. Without heatsinking, the current may need to be derated to prevent thermal shutdown. The integrated current limiting protects the driver and motor from overcurrent conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: The 2A rating is suitable for most small to medium DC motors. For higher currents, consider parallel operation or contact our FAE team for alternatives.

faqs.question: How do I control motor speed with the PSD8826?
faqs.answer: Motor speed is controlled using PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signals applied to the enable pins. The duty cycle of the PWM signal determines the average voltage applied to the motor, which controls speed. The PSD8826 supports PWM frequencies up to 100 kHz. For smooth speed control, use frequencies above 20 kHz to avoid audible noise. The device includes internal shoot-through protection to prevent simultaneous conduction of high-side and low-side switches. Connect the PWM signal to the EN pin while using the IN pins to control direction.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20-50 kHz PWM for quiet operation. Our FAE team can provide reference code for microcontroller-based motor control.

faqs.question: How does the integrated current sensing work?
faqs.answer: The PSD8826 includes integrated current sensing using a 0.2Ω sense resistor in the low-side return path. The voltage across this resistor is proportional to motor current and can be monitored at the ISENSE pin. This eliminates the need for external sense resistors and associated power loss. The current signal can be used for closed-loop current control, overcurrent protection, or stall detection. For best accuracy, use a differential measurement or ensure the sense pin reference is properly grounded.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated sensing for current limiting and protection. For precise current control, calibrate against a known current source.

faqs.question: Can the PSD8826 drive a stepper motor?
faqs.answer: Yes, the PSD8826 can drive a bipolar stepper motor by using both H-bridges together. Connect one bridge to each phase of the stepper motor. Control the phase currents using the PWM inputs to implement microstepping. While the PSD8826 doesn't include built-in stepper control logic, it provides the power stage for external controllers. For full-step or half-step operation, use the IN pins to sequence the phases. For microstepping, use PWM to control current levels in each phase.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use dedicated stepper drivers for complex microstepping. The PSD8826 is best for simple full/half-step applications or DC motor control.

faqs.question: What protection features does the PSD8826 include?
faqs.answer: The PSD8826 includes comprehensive protection features: Overcurrent Protection (OCP) limits current to safe levels during overload; Thermal Shutdown disables the driver at 165°C junction temperature with hysteresis; Short-Circuit Protection prevents damage from motor terminal shorts; Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) disables operation when supply voltage is too low; and Shoot-Through Protection prevents simultaneous conduction of high-side and low-side switches. These features protect both the driver and connected motor under fault conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: The built-in protections are sufficient for most applications. For additional protection, add external TVS diodes for transient suppression.

products.name: 3-Phase BLDC Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Three-phase BLDC motor driver with sensorless control, 5A output current, integrated gate drivers
descriptionParagraphs: The PSB8060 is a high-performance three-phase brushless DC motor driver with integrated sensorless control algorithms.
Capable of delivering up to 5A continuous output current, this driver is ideal for high-efficiency motor applications in industrial and automotive systems.
The device features field-oriented control (FOC), space vector PWM, adjustable speed and torque control, and comprehensive protection features.

faeReview.author: Kevin Liu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Motion Control
faeReview.content: The PSB8066 is an excellent choice for high-performance BLDC applications. The integrated sensorless control eliminates the need for Hall sensors, reducing system cost and complexity. I've used this driver in pump applications where the field-oriented control provides smooth, efficient operation across a wide speed range. The 95% efficiency significantly reduces thermal management requirements compared to traditional control methods. The SPI interface allows real-time parameter adjustment and diagnostics. For best performance, I recommend using low-RDS(on) external MOSFETs and implementing proper current sensing for the FOC algorithm.
faeReview.highlight: Integrated sensorless FOC with high efficiency for BLDC motors

alternativeParts.comparison: PSB8060=><DRV8313: Output current 3A < 5A (PrideSilicon higher current), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Simpler alternative with integrated power stage
alternativeParts.useCase: Suitable for basic BLDC applications without FOC requirements

alternativeParts.comparison: PSB8060=><STSPIN32F0: Output current 1.3A < 5A (PrideSilicon higher current), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.useCase: Best for compact designs requiring minimal external components

faqs.question: What is sensorless BLDC control and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Sensorless BLDC control eliminates the need for Hall effect sensors by detecting rotor position through back-EMF sensing. The PSB8060 measures the voltage induced in the unenergized motor phase to determine rotor position. This information is used to commutate the motor at the optimal time for maximum efficiency. Sensorless control reduces system cost, eliminates sensor wiring, and improves reliability. The technique works well at medium to high speeds; at very low speeds, the back-EMF is too small for reliable detection, requiring special startup algorithms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Sensorless control is ideal for cost-sensitive and high-reliability applications. For very low-speed operation, consider sensored control or special startup algorithms.

faqs.question: What is field-oriented control (FOC) and what are its benefits?
faqs.answer: Field-oriented control (FOC) is an advanced motor control technique that transforms three-phase currents into a rotating reference frame aligned with the rotor flux. This allows independent control of torque and flux, similar to DC motor control. Benefits include: 1) Maximum torque per ampere for highest efficiency; 2) Smooth operation across entire speed range; 3) Fast dynamic response to load changes; 4) Precise speed and position control; 5) Lower torque ripple and noise. The PSB8060 implements FOC internally, simplifying system design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use FOC for high-performance applications requiring efficiency and smooth operation. Contact our FAE team for FOC parameter tuning guidance.

faqs.question: How do I select external MOSFETs for the PSB8060?
faqs.answer: External MOSFET selection depends on voltage, current, and switching requirements. Key parameters: 1) VDS rating should be at least 1.5x the maximum supply voltage; 2) RDS(on) should be low enough to handle current without excessive heating; 3) Gate charge affects switching speed and driver power dissipation; 4) Body diode characteristics affect commutation losses. For a 24V system with 5A current, select MOSFETs with VDS >= 60V and RDS(on) < 20mΩ. The PSB8060's 1A gate drive capability supports fast switching of large MOSFETs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select MOSFETs with adequate voltage margin and low RDS(on). Our FAE team can recommend specific parts for your voltage and current requirements.

faqs.question: What control interfaces does the PSB8060 support?
faqs.answer: The PSB8060 supports multiple control interfaces: SPI for full parameter access and diagnostics including speed, torque, and current limits; PWM for simple speed control with duty cycle determining speed; Analog voltage input for speed control using a potentiometer or DAC; and Digital inputs for direction and enable control. The SPI interface provides the most flexibility, allowing real-time adjustment of control parameters and access to diagnostic information including motor current, speed, and fault status.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI for advanced applications requiring parameter adjustment. Use PWM for simple speed control applications. Contact our FAE team for interface selection guidance.

faqs.question: How do I tune the FOC parameters for my motor?
faqs.answer: FOC tuning involves setting current loop PI gains and speed loop PI gains. Start with current loop tuning: 1) Set proportional gain (Kp) to achieve fast response without oscillation; 2) Set integral gain (Ki) to eliminate steady-state error. Then tune speed loop with lower gains than current loop. The PSB8060 includes auto-tuning features to simplify this process. Provide motor parameters (resistance, inductance, back-EMF constant) and the algorithm calculates initial gains. Fine-tune based on actual performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use auto-tuning first, then fine-tune for optimal performance. Our FAE team can provide detailed tuning procedures and motor parameter measurement guidance.

products.name: Quad Half-Bridge Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Quad half-bridge driver for stepper and DC motors with 2A per channel and microstepping support
descriptionParagraphs: The PSD6204 is a quad half-bridge motor driver capable of driving two stepper motors or four DC motors.
With 2A per channel and low RDS(on) of 0.25Ω, this driver provides efficient motor control for robotics, automation, and CNC applications.
The device supports full-step, half-step, and microstepping modes with integrated current regulation and decay control.

faeReview.author: Robert Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Control
faeReview.content: The PSD6204 is an excellent choice for stepper motor applications requiring microstepping. The integrated current regulation and decay control provide smooth, quiet operation. I've used this driver in 3D printer designs where the microstepping capability up to 1/16 step produces smooth motion and high-quality prints. The 0.25Ω RDS(on) keeps power dissipation low even at 2A continuous. The SPI interface allows real-time configuration of current levels and decay modes. For best thermal performance, I recommend using thermal vias under the package and adequate copper area for heat spreading.
faeReview.highlight: Quad driver with microstepping and integrated current regulation

products.name: Single-Phase BLDC Pre-Driver
products.shortDescription: Single-phase BLDC motor pre-driver with sensorless control, 10A gate drive for high-power fans and pumps
descriptionParagraphs: The PSB4010 is a single-phase BLDC motor pre-driver designed for high-power cooling fans, blowers, and pump applications.
With 10A gate drive capability and advanced sensorless control algorithms, this pre-driver supports motors up to 200W.
The device features sinusoidal commutation, speed control via PWM or analog input, and comprehensive protection features.

faeReview.author: Kevin Liu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Motion Control
faeReview.content: The PSB4010 is specifically designed for high-power single-phase BLDC applications. The 10A gate drive capability supports large MOSFETs for motors up to 200W. I've used this pre-driver in industrial cooling systems where the sinusoidal commutation provides quiet, efficient operation. The sensorless control eliminates Hall sensors, improving reliability. The I2C interface allows fine-tuning of commutation parameters for optimal efficiency. For best performance, I recommend using low-RDS(on) MOSFETs and implementing proper current sensing for protection. The soft-start function reduces inrush current during startup.
faeReview.highlight: 10A gate drive with sinusoidal commutation for high-power BLDC

categories.name: Sensor Interfaces
categories.description: Precision operational amplifiers and instrumentation amplifiers for sensor signal conditioning
categories.longDescription: PrideSilicon's sensor interface portfolio provides precision amplification and signal conditioning for a wide range of sensor applications. Our operational amplifiers and instrumentation amplifiers feature low offset, low noise, and excellent stability to meet the requirements of precision measurement systems. The product line includes precision op-amps for general-purpose amplification, instrumentation amplifiers for differential sensor interfaces, and low-noise amplifiers for sensitive applications. All sensor interface ICs support wide supply voltage ranges and offer rail-to-rail input and output capability. Key features include offset voltage as low as 10uV, noise density down to 5nV/√Hz, bandwidth up to 10MHz, and industrial and automotive temperature grades. As an authorized PrideSilicon distributor, we provide comprehensive technical support including reference designs, application notes, and signal conditioning design services to help you achieve optimal system performance.

selectionGuide.title: Sensor Interface Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right amplifier for your sensor application
recommendations: Use instrumentation amps for differential sensors

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right PrideSilicon sensor interface?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting PrideSilicon amplifiers based on sensor type, gain requirements, and noise specifications

faqs.question: What types of sensor interface ICs does PrideSilicon offer?
faqs.answer: PrideSilicon offers two main types of sensor interface ICs: Precision Operational Amplifiers for general-purpose signal amplification with low offset, low noise, and rail-to-rail operation; and Instrumentation Amplifiers for differential sensor interfaces with high common-mode rejection, precise gain setting, and excellent DC accuracy. Op-amps are ideal for single-ended sensors and general amplification. Instrumentation amplifiers excel in bridge sensors, strain gauges, and applications requiring high common-mode rejection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose op-amps for single-ended sensors and general amplification. Choose instrumentation amplifiers for differential sensors and high CMRR applications. Contact our FAE team for selection guidance.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the gain for my sensor amplifier?
faqs.answer: Amplifier gain is calculated based on sensor output range and ADC input range. The formula is: Gain = ADC_Full_Scale / Sensor_Output_Range. For example, if a load cell outputs ±10mV and the ADC accepts ±2.5V, the required gain is 250. Include margin to avoid clipping at maximum sensor output. For op-amps, gain is set using external resistors: Gain = 1 + Rf/Rin. For instrumentation amplifiers, gain is often set with a single resistor: Gain = 1 + (100kΩ/Rg) for typical configurations. Consider gain bandwidth product when selecting amplifier.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required gain based on sensor output and ADC input ranges. Contact our FAE team for gain stage design and noise optimization.

faqs.question: What is CMRR and why is it important for sensor applications?
faqs.answer: CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) measures an amplifier's ability to reject signals that appear equally on both inputs. It is expressed in dB, with higher values indicating better rejection. High CMRR is critical for sensor applications because sensors often operate in noisy environments with significant common-mode interference. PrideSilicon instrumentation amplifiers achieve CMRR of 100dB or higher, meaning they reject common-mode noise by a factor of 100,000. This allows accurate measurement of small differential signals in the presence of large common-mode voltages.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select amplifiers with CMRR >80dB for industrial applications. Use instrumentation amplifiers for highest CMRR in noisy environments.

faqs.question: How do I minimize noise in sensor amplifier circuits?
faqs.answer: Minimizing noise in sensor circuits involves several techniques: 1) Select low-noise amplifiers with noise density appropriate for your signal level; 2) Keep source resistance low to minimize thermal noise; 3) Use proper PCB layout with short traces and good grounding; 4) Implement filtering to limit bandwidth to only what is needed; 5) Use shielding and guarding for high-impedance sensors; 6) Consider auto-zero or chopper amplifiers for DC precision. The total noise is the RMS sum of amplifier voltage noise, current noise times source resistance, and thermal noise of resistors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Analyze noise sources in your signal chain. Our FAE team can help optimize amplifier selection and circuit design for minimum noise.

faqs.question: What are the key PCB layout considerations for precision amplifiers?
faqs.answer: Proper PCB layout is critical for precision amplifier performance. Key recommendations include: 1) Use a solid ground plane under the amplifier and sensor connections; 2) Keep input traces short and symmetric for differential inputs; 3) Place decoupling capacitors (0.1uF ceramic) close to power pins; 4) Separate analog and digital ground planes, connecting at a single point; 5) Use guard rings around high-impedance inputs to prevent leakage; 6) Keep high-current traces away from sensitive analog signals. Following these guidelines ensures optimal DC accuracy and noise performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow layout guidelines for precision performance. Our FAE team can review your PCB layout and provide optimization recommendations.

products.name: Precision Operational Amplifier
products.shortDescription: Low-offset precision op-amp with 10uV offset, rail-to-rail I/O, 2MHz bandwidth for sensor applications
descriptionParagraphs: The PSA2001 is a precision operational amplifier featuring ultra-low offset voltage of 10uV and rail-to-rail input and output operation.
With low noise of 15nV/√Hz and 2MHz bandwidth, this amplifier is ideal for sensor signal conditioning and precision measurement applications.
The device operates from single or dual supplies, features low input bias current of 50pA, and excellent stability with capacitive loads.

faeReview.author: Lisa Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Analog
faeReview.content: The PSA2001 is my top choice for precision sensor applications. The 10uV offset voltage is exceptional for a general-purpose op-amp, eliminating the need for external trimming in many applications. I've used this amplifier in thermocouple measurement circuits where the low offset drift of 0.05uV/°C ensures stable readings across temperature. The rail-to-rail input is particularly useful for single-supply sensor applications where the input can approach the supply rails. The low bias current of 50pA makes it suitable for high-impedance sensors like pH electrodes. For best DC performance, I recommend using a solid ground plane and keeping input traces short and symmetric.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low offset with rail-to-rail operation for precision sensors

alternativeParts.reason: Lower offset alternative for highest DC precision
alternativeParts.useCase: Best for DC applications requiring lowest offset

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative for less demanding applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Suitable for general-purpose applications with cost constraints

companionParts.description: ADC for digitizing amplified sensor signal
LDO for clean analog power supply

faqs.question: What is the input offset voltage and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Input offset voltage is the differential voltage that must be applied to the input terminals to force the output to zero. The PSA2001 has a maximum offset of 10uV, which is excellent for precision applications. Offset voltage is important because it directly affects DC accuracy - for a gain of 100, a 10uV offset becomes 1mV at the output. This error adds to the sensor signal and cannot be distinguished from real data. Low offset is critical for sensors with small output signals like thermocouples (40uV/°C) and strain gauges (2mV/V).
faqs.decisionGuide: Select amplifiers with offset voltage less than 1% of your minimum signal. The PSA2001 is excellent for millivolt-level signals.

faqs.question: How does the rail-to-rail input stage work?
faqs.answer: Rail-to-rail input allows the amplifier to accept input voltages that extend to or beyond the power supply rails. The PSA2001 uses a complementary input stage with both PMOS and NMOS transistors. The PMOS pair operates near the negative rail, while the NMOS pair operates near the positive rail. This provides continuous operation across the entire supply range. Rail-to-rail input is essential for single-supply applications where the sensor output may approach ground or the positive supply, such as bridge sensors powered from the same supply as the amplifier.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use rail-to-rail input amplifiers for single-supply sensor applications. The PSA2001 handles inputs to both supply rails.

faqs.question: What is the significance of input bias current?
faqs.answer: Input bias current is the small current that flows into or out of the amplifier input terminals due to the base or gate current of the input transistors. The PSA2001 has a very low bias current of 50pA, making it suitable for high-impedance sources. Bias current flowing through source resistance creates an offset voltage (V_error = I_bias × R_source). For a 1MΩ source resistance, 50pA bias current creates only 50uV error. High bias current can also cause errors when charging input capacitance, affecting settling time in multiplexed systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select amplifiers with bias current at least 100x lower than your signal current. The PSA2001 is excellent for high-impedance sensors.

faqs.question: How do I compensate for offset voltage in critical applications?
faqs.answer: For applications requiring offset compensation: 1) Use chopper-stabilized or auto-zero amplifiers for lowest offset; 2) Implement software calibration by measuring offset with known zero input and subtracting from readings; 3) Use AC coupling with high-pass filtering for applications where DC information is not needed; 4) Implement hardware trimming using potentiometer or digital potentiometer; 5) Use differential measurement with reference channel to cancel common errors. The PSA2001's low intrinsic offset often eliminates the need for external compensation.
faqs.decisionGuide: The PSA2001's 10uV offset is sufficient for most applications. For ultra-precision, consider software calibration or chopper amplifiers.

faqs.question: What is the gain bandwidth product and how does it affect my circuit?
faqs.answer: Gain bandwidth product (GBW) is the frequency at which the amplifier's open-loop gain drops to unity. For the PSA2001 with 2MHz GBW, the available bandwidth at a closed-loop gain of 10 is 200kHz (2MHz/10). Higher gain results in lower bandwidth. GBW determines the maximum frequency that can be amplified at a given gain. For sensor applications, ensure the amplifier bandwidth exceeds the signal bandwidth by at least 5-10x for flat frequency response. The PSA2001's 2MHz GBW supports gains up to 100 for 20kHz signals.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required GBW based on signal bandwidth and gain. The PSA2001 supports gains up to 100 for audio-frequency signals.

products.name: Low-Noise Instrumentation Amplifier
products.shortDescription: Low-noise instrumentation amplifier with high CMRR, single-resistor gain setting, excellent DC accuracy
descriptionParagraphs: The PSA2002 is a precision instrumentation amplifier featuring high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) of 120dB and low noise of 8nV/√Hz.
With single-resistor gain programming from 1 to 1000 and excellent DC accuracy, this amplifier is ideal for bridge sensors, strain gauges, and differential sensor interfaces.
The device features low offset voltage of 25uV, low offset drift of 0.1uV/°C, and rail-to-rail output for maximum dynamic range.
features: Reference pin for output level shifting

faeReview.author: Thomas Wu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Sensor Systems
faeReview.content: The PSA2002 is my top recommendation for bridge sensor applications. The 120dB CMRR effectively rejects common-mode noise in industrial environments, allowing accurate measurement of small differential signals. I've used this amplifier in weigh scale designs where the single-resistor gain setting simplifies calibration - just change one resistor to adjust the scale range. The low noise of 8nV/√Hz is excellent for instrumentation amplifiers and supports high-resolution measurement. The reference pin is useful for level-shifting the output to match ADC input ranges. For best CMRR, I recommend using balanced source resistances and keeping input traces symmetric.
faeReview.highlight: High CMRR with simple single-resistor gain programming

alternativeParts.reason: Alternative with similar CMRR but higher offset
alternativeParts.useCase: Suitable for applications where offset is less critical

companionParts.description: ADC for digitizing amplified bridge signal
LDO for clean analog power and reference
Load cell for weigh scale applications

faqs.question: How do I set the gain of the PSA2002?
faqs.answer: The PSA2002 gain is set using a single external resistor connected between the RG pins. The gain formula is: Gain = 1 + (100kΩ/Rg). For example, for a gain of 100, use Rg = 1.01kΩ. For a gain of 1000, use Rg = 100Ω. Use 0.1% tolerance resistors for accurate gain. The gain can be changed dynamically using a digital potentiometer or analog switch to select different resistors. The gain setting affects bandwidth - higher gains result in lower bandwidth (gain-bandwidth product is constant at approximately 800kHz).
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate Rg based on required gain using the formula. Use precision resistors for accurate gain. Contact our FAE team for programmable gain configurations.

faqs.question: What is the reference pin used for?
faqs.answer: The reference (REF) pin allows level-shifting of the output voltage. The output voltage is referenced to the voltage applied at the REF pin rather than ground. This is useful for: 1) Matching the ADC input range (e.g., shifting to 2.5V for a 0-5V ADC); 2) Implementing differential output by driving REF with an inverted signal; 3) Rejecting common-mode offsets in the signal chain. The REF pin should be driven by a low-impedance source, typically a voltage reference or op-amp buffer. For best CMRR, keep the REF pin quiet and well-decoupled.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use REF pin to center output in ADC range. Buffer the reference voltage with an op-amp for best performance.

faqs.question: How does the CMRR vary with frequency?
faqs.answer: CMRR typically decreases with increasing frequency due to parasitic capacitances in the input stage. The PSA2002 maintains 120dB CMRR at DC and low frequencies, decreasing to approximately 80dB at 10kHz. This is still excellent rejection for most applications. For high-frequency common-mode noise, additional external filtering may be needed. The CMRR is also affected by source impedance imbalance - ensure both input source resistances are matched for best CMRR. A 1% imbalance in source resistance can reduce CMRR by 40dB.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match source impedances for best CMRR. Add external filtering for high-frequency common-mode noise. Contact our FAE team for high-CMRR design techniques.

faqs.question: Can I use the PSA2002 with single supply?
faqs.answer: Yes, the PSA2002 operates from single supplies of 2.7V to 5.5V. For single-supply operation, the inputs must be biased within the common-mode range (typically 0.5V to Vcc-0.5V). Bridge sensors powered from the same supply naturally satisfy this requirement. The output can swing rail-to-rail, maximizing dynamic range. Use the REF pin to set the output quiescent level, typically at mid-supply (Vcc/2) for maximum symmetrical swing. Ensure the reference voltage is well-decoupled and stable.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use single supply for battery-powered applications. Bias REF at mid-supply for maximum output swing. Contact our FAE team for single-supply design guidance.

faqs.question: What are the key differences between an instrumentation amplifier and a standard op-amp difference amplifier?
faqs.answer: Instrumentation amplifiers (INAs) offer several advantages over op-amp difference amplifiers: 1) Much higher input impedance (GΩ vs kΩ) eliminates loading on sensor bridges; 2) Better CMRR (120dB vs 60-80dB typical) for superior noise rejection; 3) Single-resistor gain setting vs multiple resistors required for op-amp circuits; 4) Better gain accuracy (0.05% vs 1% typical) due to integrated matched resistors; 5) Lower offset drift due to integrated laser-trimmed resistors. The trade-off is higher cost and power consumption. For precision sensor applications, INAs provide superior performance with simpler design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use INAs for precision sensor interfaces requiring high CMRR and accuracy. Use op-amp difference amplifiers for cost-sensitive, less critical applications.

products.name: Zero-Drift Precision Op-Amp
products.shortDescription: Zero-drift op-amp with 1μV offset, chopper-stabilized architecture for ultra-precision DC applications
descriptionParagraphs: The PSA3001 is a zero-drift precision operational amplifier using chopper-stabilized architecture to achieve ultra-low offset voltage.
With 1μV maximum offset and 0.01μV/°C drift, this amplifier is ideal for high-precision DC measurement, strain gauges, and thermocouple interfaces.
The device features rail-to-rail input and output, 2MHz bandwidth, and excellent EMI rejection for industrial environments.

faeReview.author: Lisa Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Analog
faeReview.content: The PSA3001 is my top recommendation for ultra-precision DC applications. The chopper-stabilized architecture eliminates 1/f noise and achieves near-zero offset drift. I've used this amplifier in precision weigh scales where the 1μV offset and 0.01μV/°C drift ensure accurate measurements across temperature. The lack of 1/f noise makes it ideal for low-frequency signals like thermocouples. The 130dB CMRR provides excellent rejection of common-mode interference. For best performance, I recommend placing filtering capacitors close to the supply pins and using a solid ground plane. Note that chopper amplifiers have clock feedthrough that may need filtering in sensitive applications.
faeReview.highlight: 1μV offset with near-zero drift for ultra-precision DC applications

products.name: High-Voltage Instrumentation Amplifier
products.shortDescription: High-voltage instrumentation amplifier with ±60V input range, 100dB CMRR for industrial sensor interfaces
descriptionParagraphs: The PSI8200 is a high-voltage instrumentation amplifier featuring ±60V input common-mode range and exceptional 100dB CMRR.
Designed for industrial sensor interfaces, this amplifier can handle high common-mode voltages while accurately amplifying small differential signals.
The device offers single-resistor gain programming from 1 to 1000, rail-to-rail output, and robust protection features.

faeReview.author: Thomas Wu
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Sensor Systems
faeReview.content: The PSI8200 is specifically designed for high-voltage industrial applications where large common-mode voltages are present. The ±60V input range allows direct connection to high-voltage buses without attenuation. I've used this amplifier in motor current monitoring applications where the common-mode voltage swings between 0V and 48V. The 100dB CMRR ensures accurate current measurement despite the high common-mode voltage. The overvoltage protection prevents damage from transients. For best performance, I recommend using balanced source resistances and keeping input traces symmetric. The wide supply range provides flexibility for various industrial systems.
faeReview.highlight: ±60V input range with 100dB CMRR for high-voltage applications
